What are Weingarten Rights… and why should nurses know them?
You have the right to representation during certain workplace situations, and a lot of nurses don’t even realize it.
Knowing your rights isn’t optional. It’s how you protect your license, your job, and yourself.

NDRIs: different from SSRIs & SNRIs 💊🧠
NDRI = Norepinephrine + Dopamine Reuptake Inhibitor
(Think bupropion.)
Remember:
✔️ Used for depression & smoking cessation
✔️ NO sexual dysfunction (common test point 👀)
✔️ Can increase risk of seizures
✔️ Avoid in patients with eating d

If you're constantly mixing up insulin onset, peak, and duration, try thinking of them like runners in a race:
 
🏃‍♀️ Rapid-acting = the sprinter (starts fast, finishes fast)
🏃 Regular = the steady jogger

Why you can't stop blood pressure meds cold turkey ❌🫀
 
When blood pressure medication is stopped abruptly instead of being tapered, the pressure doesn't simply return to baseline—it snaps right past it. Educate your patients to always taper slowly.
 
Save this one for your NCLE

Adult Defibrillator Pad Placement 101 ⚡🫀
 
Always follow the diagram on your pads + your facility protocol!
 
#NursingSchool #CodeBlueReady #defibrillatorbasics #nurselife #codeblue

Neuro medications for nursing school, made easy 🧠
 
Benzodiazepines bring everything down, triptans tighten cranial vessels, and phenytoin stops seizure activity. Save this before your exam ✨

🦵They’re small and deep
🦵Pts foot may have swelling or poor circulation

Types of needles you need to know before clinicals:

💉Blunt filter needle: draws up meds from glass ampules + filters out glass particles (never used for injection)
💉Blunt fill needle: draws from vials only, never used for injection
💉Injection needle: IM and SubQ injections

ECG Interpretation Quiz for Nursing School Students,  Nurses,  and more

Tall, peaked t waves are seen with which electrolyte imbalance: 
A. hypercalcemia
B. hyperkalemia
C. hypocalcemia
D. hypokalemia

Answer is B: Hyperkalemia. This means there’s too much potassium in the blood

NCLEX® brain is real. One minute you feel ready, and the next you’re second-guessing every answer choice.
That’s why having the right study plan matters. Archer Review helps you build confidence with NCLEX®-style questions, readiness assessments, rationales, and support to help y

🧠 Why do babies go through withdrawal after birth? Here's the analogy that makes it click.
When opioids cross the placenta during pregnancy, baby's nervous system adapts — they get used to having them. Then the cord is cut, the supply stops, and the volume slams to max. 😳
That

Being ready for the NCLEX® doesn’t always look loud.
It’s easy to see people online sharing how many hours they studied, how many questions they did, or how confident they feel. But your prep doesn’t have to look like anyone else’s to be real.
Quiet consistency counts. Reviewing

Rachel is breaking down 7 SATA tips future nurses need to know in this quick cheat sheet sneak peek! 👀
Want more must-know nursing tips like this to help you succeed on your nursing school exams and the NCLEX? You’ll find tons of cheat sheets (and so much more) waiting for you w

🚨 Adrenal Crisis Nursing Priorities
✅ Rapidly recognize the signs
✅ Replace volume → get that BP up
✅ Correct sodium & glucose
✅ Give stress-dose IV hydrocortisone
Adrenal crisis happens when the body doesn’t have enough cortisol to respond to stress. Without prompt treatment, p

🚨 "This is not normal for her at all" — and that's when your nurse brain should kick in.
Flushed skin. Fever. Tachycardia. Tremors. Hyperreflexia.
This isn't just anxiety. This is serotonin syndrome — and it can happen fast when meds get stacked.
SSRIs + Tramadol = a setup you n

🚗 Red blood cells are the Uber drivers of your body.
Their job? Deliver oxygen to every tissue and organ that needs it.
When anemia hits, those drivers start disappearing—and oxygen delivery slows down. The result? Fatigue, weakness, shortness of breath, and tissues that aren’t

Halo devices require careful monitoring, and understanding potential complications is an important part of neuro and orthopedic nursing care. Memory tools like this can make it easier to organize the key risks associated with halo immobilization.
Use this quick review to reinforc

📚 Placenta Previa vs. Abruption
🔹 Previa = Location problem
Placenta covers the cervix → bright red, painless bleeding
🔹 Abruption = Attachment problem
Placenta detaches → dark red bleeding, tender uterus, fetal distress
Remember:
Previa = Wrong location
Abruption = Wrong sepa
Don’t self doubt while taking the NCLEX - you got this! #archernursing #nclex #nursetok

Shelley’s nursing journey began right after getting married—and like many paths in nursing, it wasn’t linear.
After working in pediatrics, she stepped away to raise her four kids. Coming back? Intimidating. Full of doubt. Wondering if she still had what it takes.
But step by step

Nursing isn’t a perfect journey.
There are moments you question yourself. Moments you feel like you’re not cut out for it.
But those moments? They’re part of becoming the nurse you’re meant to be.
We see it every day—and we’re here for it.
Keep going. You’ve got this. 💫
#ArcherR

Sometimes your path into nursing starts with something personal.
For Morgan, it began by supporting a loved one—and grew into a career built on purpose, progress, and impact.
From working as a CNA in long-term care to earning a doctorate in nursing, each step came with challenges
